that can be attached to any object (class, method, property) using well-known keys. Angular's DI container reads this metadata to determine how to construct services and components.

Angular Internals — How DI Uses Reflect

keyed on the target object. Because the target is the actual class constructor, the metadata is tied to the class's lifetime — when the class is garbage collected, the metadata is too.

Angular 17+ applications using the TC39 stage-3 decorators and standalone components no longer require reflect-metadata or emitDecoratorMetadata. The new inject() function replaces constructor injection and reads metadata differently. For new projects on Angular 17+, prefer inject() over constructor injection and avoid adding reflect-metadata manually.
Building a Mini DI Container with Reflect

, the TypeScript compiler emits the TypeScript type of each
. This lets runtime code inspect types that would otherwise be erased.

design:type can only reliably capture primitive wrappers (String, Number, Boolean) and class constructor references. Interface types, union types (string | null), tuple types, and generic parameters all emit Object — they have no runtime representation.

content: Use Symbol keys to avoid collisions between your metadata and Angular's or other libraries' metadata.
Angular's DI reads design:paramtypes at injection time to determine which services to inject into constructors.
In Angular 17+ standalone apps, prefer inject() over reflect-metadata-based constructor injection.
